reflection.theme: The Friday That Used to Belong to Someone Else
reflection.quote: It is not the man who has too little, but the man who craves more, that is poor.
reflection.reading:
Fridays used to be a starting gun. The week ended and the real project began — chasing a feeling I could never quite catch, then chasing it again the next weekend because it always slipped. I told myself this was freedom. Looking back, it was the opposite. My whole week was just the runway for two days of forgetting myself, and Monday morning was a small funeral.

The Friday I have now is quieter and, somehow, much bigger. I get to feel the actual end of a workweek instead of erasing it. I get to make plans I'll remember. I get to wake up tomorrow with the morning intact — coffee that tastes like coffee, a body that works, a phone I'm not scared to look at. Seneca knew something about this. The problem was never that I didn't have enough; the problem was that more was the only direction I knew how to move.

If you're new to this, the first sober Friday nights feel strange. Like the room is too quiet, like everyone else got an invitation you didn't. Sit with it. That hollow feeling isn't sobriety being boring — it's the chase finally stopping, and you not knowing yet what to do with your hands. You'll figure it out. The life on the other side of that quiet is the one you actually wanted.
reflection.meditation: Today I choose presence over the chase. The evening ahead belongs to me.

affirmations: I am building a life I don't need to escape from.
Today I trust that quiet is not the same as empty.
I have the capacity to enjoy a Friday night sober — and to remember it tomorrow.

gratitude.prayer: Grateful for another Friday I get to end on my own terms. Grateful for the friends who don't need me drinking to want me around, for a body that recovered from years I wasn't kind to it, and for the small luxury of looking forward to the morning. The chase cost me a lot. Not chasing has given me more.
gratitude.closing: One day at a time — and today, that's enough.
